items.prompt_md: In the weighted-grid variant of Dijkstra used for swim-in-rising-water, what replaces the usual `distance + edge_weight` when pushing a neighbor onto the heap?
options: `max(current path bottleneck, neighbor's cell value)`
`distance + neighbor's cell value`
`distance - neighbor's cell value`
Just the neighbor's cell value, ignoring the current path
items.answer: `max(current path bottleneck, neighbor's cell value)`
items.explanation_md: The quantity being minimized is the worst single cell crossed, not a running sum, so combining means taking the larger of the path's bottleneck so far and the new cell.

items.prompt_md: Why is plain BFS wrong for finding the minimum-bottleneck path across a weighted grid?
options: BFS counts hops, not the cost baked into each cell, so a short-looking path could still force you through one very high-cost cell without BFS ever noticing
BFS can't traverse two-dimensional grids at all
BFS requires the grid to be square
BFS only works when there's a single row
items.answer: BFS counts hops, not the cost baked into each cell, so a short-looking path could still force you through one very high-cost cell without BFS ever noticing
items.explanation_md: BFS's queue order reflects hop count, which only matches true cost when every move costs the same — grid cell values break that assumption.

items.prompt_md: With an n x n grid (n^2 cells, about 4n^2 edges), what is the time complexity of the heap-based traversal shown in the lesson?
options: O(n^2 log n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n^2 log n)
items.explanation_md: This is Dijkstra's O((V + E) log V) bound with V = n^2 and E = O(n^2) (each cell has up to 4 edges), giving O(n^2 log n).

items.prompt_md: Even though `max` replaces `+` as the combine rule, why does "the first time the destination is popped, that value is optimal" still hold?
options: The heap still always pops the globally smallest tentative value next; any not-yet-explored route to the destination must be from an entry still in the heap, which can only be at least as large
Because `max` and `+` always produce identical numbers on a grid
Because the grid is guaranteed to have no cycles
Because there is only ever one path between any two cells in a grid
items.answer: The heap still always pops the globally smallest tentative value next; any not-yet-explored route to the destination must be from an entry still in the heap, which can only be at least as large
items.explanation_md: The pop-order argument only depends on the combine rule never decreasing a path's value as it extends — both `+` (with non-negative weights) and `max` satisfy that.

items.prompt_md: What does each heap entry represent in the weighted-grid Dijkstra shown in the lesson?
options: `(bottleneck-or-cost so far, row, col)`
`(row, col)` only, ordered by insertion
`(row * col, distance)`
A full path stored as a list of cells
items.answer: `(bottleneck-or-cost so far, row, col)`
items.explanation_md: The heap key is the path-cost quantity being minimized, with the cell coordinates as the payload identifying which node that cost belongs to.

items.prompt_md: A buggy grid-Dijkstra marks a cell as `visited` the moment it's pushed onto the heap, rather than when it's popped. What goes wrong?
options: A cheaper route to that same cell, discovered later but still sitting in the heap, gets skipped even though it hasn't actually been finalized yet, potentially locking in a worse result
Nothing — marking on push or on pop is equivalent
The heap throws an error when this happens
It makes the algorithm strictly faster with no downside
items.answer: A cheaper route to that same cell, discovered later but still sitting in the heap, gets skipped even though it hasn't actually been finalized yet, potentially locking in a worse result
items.explanation_md: Finalizing on push instead of pop breaks the same invariant that makes Dijkstra correct — a cell should only be considered "done" once its smallest heap entry has actually been popped.

items.prompt_md: What's the downside of the binary-search-over-threshold-plus-BFS approach to swim-in-rising-water, compared to the direct heap-frontier Dijkstra variant?
options: It needs two separate pieces of logic (a search over thresholds and a reachability check at each one) instead of finding the answer directly in a single pass
It's always asymptotically slower on every input
It cannot work on grids at all
It requires the grid to contain negative values
items.answer: It needs two separate pieces of logic (a search over thresholds and a reachability check at each one) instead of finding the answer directly in a single pass
items.explanation_md: Binary search plus a reachability check is O(n^2 log n) too, but the heap-frontier version gets the same bound with one self-contained pass instead of two nested procedures.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the heap-based grid traversal on an n x n grid?
options: O(n)
items.explanation_md: The visited array is O(n^2) and the heap can hold up to O(n^2) entries (a few per cell), so total space is O(n^2).

items.prompt_md: The lesson notes you can swap `+` for `max` and the algorithm stays correct. What property must a combine function have for Dijkstra's pop-order argument to still work?
options: Combining a path's running cost with a new edge's cost must never decrease the value — extending a path can only make it as expensive or more, never cheaper
The combine function must always be addition
The combine function must return a value strictly between 0 and 1
The combine function must be commutative
items.answer: Combining a path's running cost with a new edge's cost must never decrease the value — extending a path can only make it as expensive or more, never cheaper
items.explanation_md: That monotonicity is exactly what guarantees the heap's pop order matches true cost order, whether the combine rule is `+` with non-negative weights or `max`.

items.prompt_md: If every grid edge weight were restricted to only 0 or 1 (instead of arbitrary elevations), what technique could replace the min-heap and still run correctly, in better O(V + E) time?
options: 0-1 BFS: a deque where 0-weight neighbors are pushed to the front and 1-weight neighbors to the back, keeping it ordered without a heap
Plain BFS, entirely ignoring the weights
Kruskal's algorithm
Bellman-Ford relaxation for exactly one round
items.answer: 0-1 BFS: a deque where 0-weight neighbors are pushed to the front and 1-weight neighbors to the back, keeping it ordered without a heap
items.explanation_md: When weights are restricted to {0, 1}, a deque can maintain the same sorted-frontier property a heap would, but with O(1) pushes instead of O(log V).

items.prompt_md: A 2D grid where moving into a cell costs its elevation, and you must find a path from top-left to bottom-right that minimizes the highest elevation you're ever forced to cross. Which approach fits?
options: A min-heap of `(bottleneck-so-far, row, col)`, popping the smallest bottleneck and expanding to the 4 neighbors
BFS, treating every step as cost 1
Sort every cell by elevation and take the first n of them
DFS trying every possible path and taking the min at the end
items.answer: A min-heap of `(bottleneck-so-far, row, col)`, popping the smallest bottleneck and expanding to the 4 neighbors
items.explanation_md: A weighted grid where cost is baked into each cell (not the hop count) is Dijkstra territory, here with `max` in place of `+`.

items.prompt_md: A grid where every cell/move costs exactly the same (no elevation, no weight) and you need the fewest steps from start to end. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS over the grid
A min-heap of (bottleneck-so-far, row, col)
Minimum spanning tree over the grid cells
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: Plain BFS over the grid
items.explanation_md: With no per-cell weight, hop count and true cost are the same thing, so plain BFS finds the fewest steps without any heap machinery.

items.prompt_md: A grid where moving into a neighboring cell costs either 0 or 1 depending on a cell property (e.g., matching color = free, otherwise 1), and you need the minimum total cost path. Which approach fits best?
options: 0-1 BFS using a deque (push 0-cost moves to the front, 1-cost moves to the back)
Plain BFS, ignoring the 0/1 distinction entirely
Standard binary min-heap Dijkstra (still correct, but does more work than necessary here)
Kruskal's algorithm
items.answer: 0-1 BFS using a deque (push 0-cost moves to the front, 1-cost moves to the back)
items.explanation_md: Weights restricted to {0, 1} are the trigger for the 0-1 BFS deque trick — it reproduces Dijkstra's answer in linear time without heap overhead.

items.prompt_md: A grid where moving into each cell adds its energy cost to a running total (not a "worst cell" bottleneck), and you need the minimum total energy path from start to end. Which approach fits?
options: A min-heap of (total-cost-so-far, row, col), the same Dijkstra skeleton but combining with `+` instead of `max`
BFS, since grids only ever need hop counts
Minimum spanning tree via Prim's algorithm
Bounded Bellman-Ford for exactly 4 rounds
items.answer: A min-heap of (total-cost-so-far, row, col), the same Dijkstra skeleton but combining with `+` instead of `max`
items.explanation_md: This is the additive version of grid-Dijkstra: same pop-to-finalize heap skeleton, just summing cost instead of tracking a bottleneck.

items.prompt_md: A grid where you just need to know whether the end cell is reachable from the start at all — no cost, no minimization. Which approach fits?
options: Flood-fill BFS/DFS reachability
Grid Dijkstra with a min-heap tracking bottleneck cost
Prim's algorithm
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: Flood-fill BFS/DFS reachability
items.explanation_md: A yes/no reachability question on a grid is just flood-fill traversal — no weighted shortest-path algorithm is needed.

items.prompt_md: A grid where a few special cells have negative cost (a shortcut bonus), and you need the minimum total cost path from start to end. Which approach fits?
options: Treat it like Bellman-Ford: relax repeatedly, since Dijkstra's finalize-on-first-pop guarantee no longer holds with negative costs
Grid Dijkstra with a min-heap, same as the non-negative case
Plain BFS
Kruskal's algorithm
items.answer: Treat it like Bellman-Ford: relax repeatedly, since Dijkstra's finalize-on-first-pop guarantee no longer holds with negative costs
items.explanation_md: Negative cell costs are the same trigger as negative edge weights anywhere else — they break Dijkstra's greedy guarantee and call for Bellman-Ford-style repeated relaxation.

items.prompt_md: You have several isolated regions of a grid (or graph) and want to connect them all with the minimum total "bridge" cost, with no single fixed start or end. Which approach fits?
options: Minimum spanning tree (Prim's or Kruskal's) over the candidate bridge edges
Grid Dijkstra with a min-heap tracking bottleneck cost
0-1 BFS with a deque
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: Minimum spanning tree (Prim's or Kruskal's) over the candidate bridge edges
items.explanation_md: "Connect everything as cheaply as possible" with no fixed source/destination is the MST fingerprint, even when the underlying structure is a grid.

items.prompt_md: You need the k-th smallest elevation value anywhere in the grid — no path, no start/end, no traversal at all. Which approach fits?
options: A heap (or quickselect) over the flattened grid values
Grid Dijkstra with bottleneck combine
0-1 BFS
Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: A heap (or quickselect) over the flattened grid values
items.explanation_md: With no path or traversal involved — just "the k-th smallest value in a collection" — a plain heap or quickselect solves it directly.

items.prompt_md: A weighted grid where you're also capped at using at most k moves total to reach the end, and cost still matters. Which approach fits, given the cap breaks Dijkstra's guarantee?
options: Bounded relaxation (Bellman-Ford style), relaxing possible moves for at most k rounds
Plain grid Dijkstra with a min-heap, ignoring the cap
Minimum spanning tree via Prim's algorithm
Flood-fill BFS
items.answer: Bounded relaxation (Bellman-Ford style), relaxing possible moves for at most k rounds
items.explanation_md: A move/hop cap on a weighted grid is the same trigger as a stop cap on a flight network — it rules out Dijkstra and calls for bounded relaxation.

items.prompt_md: A grid of toll-booth costs, where you must find a path from entrance to exit minimizing the single highest toll paid along the way. Which approach fits?
options: A min-heap of (max-toll-so-far, row, col), popping the smallest max-toll and expanding to neighbors
Plain BFS counting the number of toll booths passed
Kruskal's algorithm over the toll booths
Bounded Bellman-Ford for a fixed number of rounds
items.answer: A min-heap of (max-toll-so-far, row, col), popping the smallest max-toll and expanding to neighbors
items.explanation_md: "Minimize the worst single value crossed" on a weighted grid is the bottleneck-Dijkstra pattern, whether the cost represents elevation, risk, or toll.
